title: The 'If I Had More Money I Would' Trap
subtitle: A reading on the deferred life plan and why waiting for money first keeps both the money and the life on hold
excerpt: A reading on the deferred life plan — the belief that real life begins once money is sorted. Explains why deferring the things you want until you have more money tends to keep both the money and the life on hold, and what the shift looks like.

practice.whenToUse: Read when you catch yourself thinking 'when I have more money, then I'll...' and that sentence has been there for a long time.

content.text: The 'if I had more money I would' sentence is one of the most common money patterns there is. The holiday that waits for the raise. The business idea shelved until there's a runway. The thing you actually want, deferred to a future version of your finances that may or may not arrive.
Why it works as a trap

The trap has two mechanisms. The first is that deferring what you want creates the internal experience of scarcity and lack — which is the exact energetic state that keeps money away. When you tell yourself 'I can't afford this life until the money arrives,' your baseline is one of not having. That's what you're broadcasting and, functionally, that's what persists.

The second mechanism is practical: the deferred thing is often evidence of the identity you're waiting to inhabit. The holiday, the course, the studio space — these are markers of the version of yourself that already has the money. Waiting until you have it first reverses the causal order. Identity usually comes before the external reality matches it.
The difference between deferral and practicality

Not all waiting is the trap. Waiting until you've saved a deposit for a house is practical planning, not a deferred life plan. The trap is specifically when the waiting is indefinite, tied to a threshold that moves, or attached to things that could be done now in a smaller form. If the sentence has been there for three years and the threshold hasn't been reached, that's the pattern.

The shift is not about spending money you don't have. It's about releasing the internal stance of 'my life is on hold.' This might mean doing a version of the deferred thing now, at whatever scale is possible. It might mean sitting with the feeling of wanting it and allowing that to be present rather than suppressing it as unrealistic. It might mean examining what specifically feels dangerous or wrong about having it now.
The journal prompts in the stuck-on sections of this library work on this directly. The energy statements for receiving and for allowing are the practice layer.
